our_argument_summary: The P-wave shadow zone at 104°–140° is foundational evidence for a spherical, layered Earth with a liquid outer core. First explained by Oldham (1906) and Gutenberg (1913). S-waves are completely blocked, P-waves refract as PKP arrivals. The dome provides no flat-disc mechanism for angular shadow zones. WIN-064 was added in V51.0 without any dome-side derivation or discussion.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline exists for WIN-064. All seven dome raw-text files contain zero mentions of P-waves, shadow zones, or seismic data. WIN-064 appears only in V51.0 WIN count but has no supporting text in the dome's source material captured in our archive.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No validation exists — neither hardcoded nor computed. No seismic monitoring domain.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None. The dome model provides no mechanism for how a flat disc produces angular refraction patterns consistent with a spherical liquid core.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review correctly identifies this as having no dome-side monitoring or derivation.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Searched all seven dome raw-text files (01-home.txt through 07-ai-context.txt). Zero hits for P-wave, shadow zone, seismic, PKP, or WIN-064-specific monitoring. No domain in monitor infrastructure handles seismic validation. Consistent with Cycle 1 finding.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The P-wave shadow zone is foundational standard seismology dating to Oldham (1906) and Gutenberg (1913). The angular range 104°–140° and the mechanism (refraction through spherical density layers) are standard physics. The dome model adopts the observation and claims it 'matches dome geometry' without providing any alternative derivation from dome parameters.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Shadow zone first identified 1906 (Oldham), core-mantle boundary calculated 1913 (Gutenberg). Dome model added WIN-064 in V51.0 (April 2026). This is retrodiction of a 120-year-old foundational discovery.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation from dome parameters (disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=8,537 km, etc.) exists that produces the 104°–140° shadow zone. The dome site provides no ray-tracing through disc layers, no flat-disc wave propagation model, and no mechanism for angular refraction cutoffs. The angular range is adopted directly from spherical seismology.

holes_found.description: DUPLICATE TEXT: The detail_evidence contains a verbatim copy-paste duplication. Starting from 'On a flat disc 20,015 km in radius, there is no spherical liquid core to refract P-waves...' through '...WIN-064 was added in V51.0 without any explanation of how a flat disc produces this observation.' — this entire block (~4 sentences) appears twice in succession. This is an embarrassing copy-paste error that a dome defender could use to argue our review is sloppy or automatically generated without human review. The duplicated text adds ~700 characters of redundancy.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove the second occurrence of the duplicated block. The text starting at the second 'On a flat disc 20,015 km in radius, there is no spherical liquid core...' through the end should be deleted. This will reduce detail_evidence from ~2808 to ~2100 characters while losing no content.

holes_found.description: Angular range inconsistency STILL PRESENT between WIN-064 and the seismic tomography section in sections.json. WIN-064 consistently uses '104°–140°' but sections.json contains '140–103°' (reversed order AND different inner boundary — 103° vs 104°). This was flagged in Cycle 1 review and has not been fixed. Standard seismology literature uses both 103° and 104° for the inner boundary (the exact onset depends on source depth and velocity model), but we should be internally consistent. The reversed order in sections.json ('140–103°') is also non-standard.
holes_found.recommendation: Standardize to '104°–140°' throughout both wins.json and sections.json. The 104° value is more commonly cited in introductory seismology texts (Shearer, Introduction to Seismology; Stein & Wysession, An Introduction to Seismology). Fix the reversed order in sections.json.

holes_found.description: The surface distance conversion '11,600–15,600 km' is correct (computed: 11,564–15,567 km on a sphere of radius 6,371 km) but the context is slightly misleading. The text says 'On a flat disc 20,015 km in radius, angular distances of 104°–140° (corresponding to 11,600–15,600 km surface distance)' — but these surface distances are computed using spherical geometry. On a flat disc, 'angular distance' from an epicenter doesn't map to surface distance the same way. The parenthetical implicitly uses sphere math while discussing a flat disc.
holes_found.recommendation: Either clarify that these are spherical surface distances (the point being that the dome model borrows spherical concepts) or remove the parenthetical. The argument is stronger without it — the key point is that angular shadow zones require spherical refraction geometry, period.

holes_found.description: The finding text says 'Shadow zone at 104-140 deg proves spherical layered Earth with liquid core.' While scientifically accurate, 'proves' is a strong epistemic claim. The shadow zone is extremely powerful evidence that is consistent with and best explained by a spherical layered Earth, but scientific claims technically demonstrate consistency rather than proof. A dome defender could nitpick 'proves' to undermine our scientific credibility, though this is a very minor point.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider softening to 'demonstrates' or keeping 'proves' — this is a judgment call. The argument IS essentially proof-level given the precision of modern seismic tomography. No change required; noting for completeness.

stronger_arguments.description: Inner core discovery: Inge Lehmann (1936) discovered the INNER core by identifying PKiKP arrivals — P-waves refracted through a SOLID inner core within the liquid outer core. This adds another layer of spherical structure that the dome model cannot explain. The inner core boundary at 5,150 km depth was derived from shadow zone analysis on a sphere. Three concentric spherical boundaries (surface, core-mantle, inner core) are all derived from shadow zone geometry — each one independently requires spherical geometry that a flat disc cannot provide.
sources: Lehmann, I. (1936). P'. Publications du Bureau Central Séismologique International, Série A, 14: 87-115.

stronger_arguments.description: Travel-time curves (Jeffreys-Bullen tables): The complete set of seismic travel-time curves (P, S, PP, SS, PcP, ScS, PKP, PKIKP, etc.) for the entire angular distance range 0°–180° are computed from spherical ray-tracing through the PREM model and match observations to <1% across tens of thousands of earthquake-station pairs. These tables cannot be reproduced by ANY flat-disc geometry. This is not a single data point but a comprehensive system of interlocking constraints that all require spherical geometry.
sources: Kennett & Engdahl (1991, Geophysical Journal International, 105: 429-465) — ak135 travel-time tables

kernel_of_truth.description: The P-wave shadow zone IS a genuinely important and well-documented geophysical phenomenon that any serious Earth model must address. The dome model correctly identifies it as significant evidence. The angular range 104°–140° is precisely measured, and the existence of shadow zones in wave propagation is real physics. Credit is due for engaging with seismic evidence at all — many alternative cosmology models simply ignore seismology.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Acknowledging a real phenomenon is necessary but not sufficient. The shadow zone was not merely 'observed' — it was DERIVED from spherical wave propagation theory applied to a layered sphere. The 104° boundary specifically encodes the ratio of core radius to Earth radius via Snell's law on a sphere. You cannot adopt the shadow zone as evidence for a flat disc because the shadow zone's angular boundaries ARE spherical geometry. It would be like claiming a globe-shaped shadow as evidence that the casting object is flat — the shape of the evidence contradicts the shape of the model.

cycle_comparison.change_magnitude: massive — detail_evidence grew from 652 to 2808 characters (330% increase)
cycle1_fixes_not_applied: Angular range inconsistency between wins.json (104°) and sections.json (103°) — still present
Duplicate text introduced during expansion (not present in Cycle 1)
new_issues_since_cycle1: Verbatim duplicate paragraph introduced during text expansion (major)
Surface distance parenthetical uses sphere math in flat-disc context (minor)

deeper_analysis: WIN-064 has been substantially improved since Cycle 1. The Gutenberg citation was fixed (DOI removed, inline citation to Physikalische Zeitschrift), S-wave shadow and PKP arrivals were added as recommended, and the overall argument is now much stronger. However, the expansion introduced a verbatim copy-paste duplication that must be fixed — it's the kind of error that undermines credibility. The 'Std Model Explains' verdict is correct and well-supported. This WIN is actually one of the STRONGEST examples of 'Std Model Explains' in the entire review because the shadow zone isn't merely explained by standard physics — it was the foundational evidence that ESTABLISHED our knowledge of Earth's internal structure. The dome model adding this as WIN-064 in V51.0 without any derivation or mechanism is a particularly clear case of the 'adopt and relabel' pattern documented in Part 2b. The code_analysis tags (none/relabels_standard/post_hoc/not-from-dome) are all confirmed correct on re-examination.

citations_verified: Oldham 1906 — DOI 10.1144/GSL.JGS.1906.062.01-04.21 — VERIFIED. Resolves to 'The Constitution of the Interior of the Earth, as Revealed by Earthquakes' by R.D. Oldham, Quarterly Journal of the Geological Society, Vol 62, pp 456-475. Correct title, journal, year, author.
citations_unchecked: Gutenberg 1913, Physikalische Zeitschrift 14: 1217-1218 — no DOI to verify (inline citation only). Journal name, volume, and page numbers match standard references. Cannot verify without access to the 1913 journal archive, but the bibliographic details are consistent with standard seismology histories.

IRIS Global Seismographic Network (143 stations) — station count not independently verified this cycle. IRIS website lists 150+ stations as of 2024, so 143 may be slightly outdated but is in the right range.
GEOSCOPE (30 stations) — station count not independently verified this cycle. GEOSCOPE lists ~33 stations currently, so 30 is approximately correct.
